name: Tafsir al-Mizan
author: Allamah Muhammad Husayn Tabatabai
text:
# Ayahs

```arabic
الٓمٓر ۚ تِلْكَ ءَايَـٰتُ ٱلْكِتَـٰبِ ۗ وَٱلَّذِىٓ أُنزِلَ إِلَيْكَ مِن رَّبِّكَ ٱلْحَقُّ وَلَـٰكِنَّ أَكْثَرَ ٱلنَّاسِ لَا يُؤْمِنُونَ (١)
```

```english
Alif, Lam, Mim, Ra. These are the signs of the Book. That which has been sent down to you from your Lord is the truth, but most people do not believe [in it]. (1)
```

```arabic
ٱللَّهُ ٱلَّذِى رَفَعَ ٱلسَّمَـٰوَٰتِ بِغَيْرِ عَمَدٍ تَرَوْنَهَا ۖ ثُمَّ ٱسْتَوَىٰ عَلَى ٱلْعَرْشِ ۖ وَسَخَّرَ ٱلشَّمْسَ وَٱلْقَمَرَ ۖ كُلٌّ يَجْرِى لِأَجَلٍ مُّسَمًّى ۚ يُدَبِّرُ ٱلْأَمْرَ يُفَصِّلُ ٱلْـَٔايَـٰتِ لَعَلَّكُم بِلِقَآءِ رَبِّكُمْ تُوقِنُونَ (٢)
```

```english
It is Allah who raised the heavens without any pillars that you see, and then presided over the Throne. He disposed the sun and the moon, each moving for a specified term. He directs the command, [and] elaborates the signs that you may be certain of encountering your Lord. (2)
```

```arabic
وَهُوَ ٱلَّذِى مَدَّ ٱلْأَرْضَ وَجَعَلَ فِيهَا رَوَٰسِىَ وَأَنْهَـٰرًا ۖ وَمِن كُلِّ ٱلثَّمَرَٰتِ جَعَلَ فِيهَا زَوْجَيْنِ ٱثْنَيْنِ ۖ يُغْشِى ٱلَّيْلَ ٱلنَّهَارَ ۚ إِنَّ فِى ذَٰلِكَ لَـَٔايَـٰتٍ لِّقَوْمٍ يَتَفَكَّرُونَ (٣)
```

```english
It is He who has spread out the earth and set in it firm mountains and streams, and of every fruit in it He has made two kinds. He draws the night’s cover over the day. There are indeed signs in that for a people who reflect. (3)
```

```arabic
وَفِى ٱلْأَرْضِ قِطَعٌ مُّتَجَـٰوِرَٰتٌ وَجَنَّـٰتٌ مِّنْ أَعْنَـٰبٍ وَزَرْعٌ وَنَخِيلٌ صِنْوَانٌ وَغَيْرُ صِنْوَانٍ يُسْقَىٰ بِمَآءٍ وَٰحِدٍ وَنُفَضِّلُ بَعْضَهَا عَلَىٰ بَعْضٍ فِى ٱلْأُكُلِ ۚ إِنَّ فِى ذَٰلِكَ لَـَٔايَـٰتٍ لِّقَوْمٍ يَعْقِلُونَ (٤)
```

```english
In the earth are neighbouring terrains [of diverse kinds] and vineyards, farms, and date palms growing from the same root and from diverse roots, [all] irrigated by the same water, and We give some of them an advantage over others in flavour. There are indeed signs in that for people who exercise their reason. (4)
```

## (Commentary)

The purpose of the Surah is to demonstrate the truth of the Book revealed to the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم), asserting that it is the sign (آية) of his messengership. It aims to refute their saying: **لَوْ لا أُنْزِلَ عَلَيْهِ آيَةٌ مِنْ رَبِّهِ** — "Why has not a sign been sent down to him from his Lord?" (13:7), by which they alluded to the Quran, not considering it a sign. Their speech is rejected, and it is not fitting for the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) to heed it, nor for them to utter it.

This is indicated by the opening of the Surah with His saying: **وَالَّذِي أُنْزِلَ إِلَيْكَ مِنْ رَبِّكَ الْحَقُّ وَلكِنَّ أَكْثَرَ النَّاسِ لا يُؤْمِنُونَ** — "and what has been sent down to you from your Lord is the truth, but most people do not believe" (13:1), and its conclusion with His saying: **وَيَقُولُ الَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا لَسْتَ مُرْسَلاً قُلْ كَفى بِاللهِ شَهِيداً بَيْنِي وَبَيْنَكُمْ** — "The faithless say, ‘You are not an apostle.’ Say, ‘Allah suffices as a witness between me and you...’" (13:43), as well as the repeated citation of their saying: **لَوْ لا أُنْزِلَ عَلَيْهِ آيَةٌ مِنْ رَبِّهِ** — "Why has not a sign been sent down to him from his Lord?" (13:7).

The sum of the explanation, addressed to the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم), is that this Quran revealed to you is the truth (الحق) which no falsehood (باطل) can mix with. Indeed, the message of the call it contains is monotheism (التوحيد) — *the absolute oneness of Allah in His essence, attributes, and acts* — which is evidenced by the signs of the cosmos (آيات الكون), such as the raising of the heavens, the spreading of the earth, the subjugation of the sun and the moon, and all else that follows the wonders of His governance and the marvels of His decree, the Exalted.

The truth of his call is also evidenced by the accounts of past nations and their remnants; messengers came to them with manifest proofs (البينات), yet they disbelieved and denied, so Allah seized them for their sins. This is what this Book contains, and it is a sign (آية) indicating your messengership.

Their saying: **لَوْ لا أُنْزِلَ عَلَيْهِ آيَةٌ** — "Why has not a sign been sent down to him" — as an allusion to the Quran, is rejected. Firstly, because you are but a warner (منذر), and you have no authority in the matter such that they should propose such things to you. Secondly, guidance (الهداية) and misguidance (الإضلال) are not, as they imagine, within the power of signs such that they might hope for guidance from a sign they propose. Rather, that belongs to Allah, the Glorified; He leads astray whom He wills and guides whom He wills according to a wise system (نظام حكيم). As for their saying: "You are not an apostle," the testimony of Allah in His speech regarding your messengership and the evidence of the true knowledge (المعارف الحقة) contained therein is sufficient proof for you.

Among the brilliant truths mentioned in this Surah is what is contained in His saying: **أَنْزَلَ مِنَ السَّماءِ ماءً** — "He sends down water from the sky..." (13:17), and His saying: **أَلا بِذِكْرِ اللهِ تَطْمَئِنُّ الْقُلُوبُ** — "Look! The hearts find rest in Allah’s remembrance!" (13:28), and His saying: **يَمْحُوا اللهُ ما يَشاءُ وَيُثْبِتُ وَعِنْدَهُ أُمُّ الْكِتابِ** — "Allah effaces or confirms whatever He wishes and with Him is the Mother Book" (13:39), and His saying: **فَلِلَّهِ الْمَكْرُ جَمِيعاً** — "but all devising belongs to Allah" (13:42).

The Surah is entirely Meccan (مكية), as indicated by the context (سياق) of its verses and the themes they contain. It has been narrated from some that it is Meccan except for its last verse, which was revealed in Medina regarding Abdullah ibn Salam; this is attributed to al-Kalbi and Muqatil. However, this is refuted by the fact that it is the conclusion of the Surah, serving as a counterpart to what was in its opening: **وَالَّذِي أُنْزِلَ إِلَيْكَ مِنْ رَبِّكَ الْحَقُ** — "and what has been sent down to you from your Lord is the truth" (13:1).

It has also been said that the Surah is entirely Medinan (مدنية) except for two verses: **وَلَوْ أَنَّ قُرْآناً سُيِّرَتْ بِهِ الْجِبالُ** — "If only it were a Quran whereby the mountains could be moved..." (13:31) and the verse following it; this is attributed to al-Hasan, Ikrimah, and Qatadah. This is refuted by the context of the verses and their contents, which do not suit the circumstances that prevailed in Medina after the Hijrah.

It has further been said that the Medinan portion is His saying, the Exalted: **وَلا يَزالُ الَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا تُصِيبُهُمْ بِما صَنَعُوا قارِعَةٌ** — "The faithless will continue to be visited by catastrophes because of their doings..." (13:31), while the rest is Meccan. It seems the proponent of this view relied on its applicability to the early state of Islam after the Hijrah until the Conquest (الفتح). The refutation of this will become clear in the explanation of the verse's meaning.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **المر تِلْكَ آياتُ الْكِتابِ وَالَّذِي أُنْزِلَ إِلَيْكَ مِنْ رَبِّكَ الْحَقُ** — "Alif, Lam, Mim, Ra. These are the signs of the Book, and what has been sent down to you from your Lord is the truth..." (13:1). The letters with which the Surah begins are the sum of the letters that open the Surahs of **الم** (Alif, Lam, Mim) and the Surahs of **الر** (Alif, Lam, Ra), just as the knowledge (المعارف) explained in this Surah seems to be the sum of the knowledge intended in those two categories of Surahs. We hope to explain this further in what follows, if Allah the Almighty wills.

His saying: **تِلْكَ آياتُ الْكِتابِ** — "These are the signs of the Book." The apparent context of the verse and the three verses following it—given their continuity—is that they enumerate the cosmic signs (الآيات الكونية), such as the raising of the heavens, the spreading of the earth, the subjugation of the sun and the moon, and other things that indicate the monotheism (توحيد) of Allah, the Glorified, which the Noble Quran articulates and to which the true call (الدعوة الحقة) invites. It mentions that contemplating their details and reflecting upon them yields certainty (اليقين) regarding the Origin (المبدأ) and the Return (المعاد) — *the resurrection and the final judgment* — and the knowledge that what was revealed to the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) is the truth.

Thus, the apparent meaning of all this is that the "signs" (الآيات) referred to by His saying: **تِلْكَ آياتُ الْكِتابِ** are the cosmic entities and external things subjugated within the general divine system (النظام العام الإلهي). The "Book" (الكتاب) refers to the totality of the cosmos, which is, in a sense, the Preserved Tablet (اللوح المحفوظ), or it refers to the Noble Quran insofar as it contains the cosmic signs through a kind of metaphorical extension (عناية ومجاز).

Accordingly, the verse contains an allusion to two types of indication (الدلالة): natural indication (الدلالة الطبيعية) which characterizes the cosmic signs in the heavens, the earth, and what is between them; and verbal indication (الدلالة اللفظية) which characterizes the Quranic verses revealed from Him, the Exalted, to His Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم). His saying: **وَلكِنَّ أَكْثَرَ النَّاسِ لا يُؤْمِنُونَ** — "but most people do not believe" — would then be a qualification (استدراك) pertaining to both sentences: "These are the signs of the Book" and "what has been sent down to you from your Lord is the truth," not just the latter.

The meaning—and Allah knows best—is: those cosmic matters—referred to with the distal demonstrative (تلك) to indicate the loftiness of their status—are the signs of the general cosmic Book, indicating that Allah, the Glorified, is One, having no partner in His Lordship (ربوبيته). And the Quran revealed to you from your Lord is the truth, not falsehood—the definite article (اللام) in **الْحَقُ** denotes exclusivity (الحصر), implying pure truth. Thus, those are definitive signs in their indication, and this is truth in its revelation, yet most people do not believe, neither in those ontological signs (الآيات العينية) nor in this revealed truth. The tone of the speech carries a sense of blame and reproach.

It has become clear from the preceding that the definite article in **الْحَقُ** is for exclusivity, meaning that what was revealed to him is truth alone, neither false nor a mixture of truth and falsehood.

The exegetes have various and differing opinions regarding the grammatical structure of the verse and the meaning of its individual words, such as the referent of the demonstrative pronoun, the meaning of "signs" and "the Book," the sense of exclusivity in "the truth," and the identity of "most people." The most apparent and suitable view for the context of the verses is what we have presented. Whoever wishes to see the details of their opinions should consult the more extensive commentaries.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **اللهُ الَّذِي رَفَعَ السَّماواتِ بِغَيْرِ عَمَدٍ تَرَوْنَها ثُمَّ اسْتَوى عَلَى الْعَرْشِ** — "It is Allah who raised the heavens without any pillars that you may see, then He settled on the Throne..." (13:2). Al-Raghib said in *al-Mufradat*: "The pillar (العمود) is that upon which a tent relies; its plural is *'umud* (عمد) with two *dammahs* or *'amad* (عمد) with two *fathahs*." He said: "In outstretched pillars (عمد ممددة), and it is also recited as *'umud*." He said: "Without pillars that you may see (بغير عمد ترونها)." End quote. It has also been said that *'amad* (عمد) with two *fathahs* is a collective noun for *'imad* (العماد), not a plural.

The purpose of the verse is to remind of the proof of His Lordship (ربوبيته) alone, without partner. The heavens are raised without pillars to support them that your eyes can perceive. There is a functioning system (نظام), and there are a sun and a moon subjugated, running for a specified term (أجل مسمى). There must be one who maintains these matters, raising the sky, organizing the system, subjugating the sun and the moon, directing the command (يدبر الأمر), and elaborating these signs (يفصل هذه الآيات) one from another—an elaboration (تفصيلاً) in which there is hope that you may be certain of the encounter with your Lord. Thus, Allah, the Glorified, is the One maintaining what has been mentioned of raising the heavens, organizing the system, subjugating the sun and the moon, directing the command, and elaborating the signs. He, the Exalted, is the Lord of all; there is no Lord besides Him.

His saying: **الَّذِي رَفَعَ السَّماواتِ بِغَيْرِ عَمَدٍ تَرَوْنَها** — "who raised the heavens without any pillars that you may see." The raising of the heavens is their separation from the earth—a separation by which they dominate the earth by casting their rays and sending down their rains, thunderbolts, and so on. They are raised above the earth without pillars perceptible to man upon which they rely. Thus, man should realize that they have a Raiser who preserves them from shifting from their place and holds them from vanishing from their station.

This is because the stability of the heavens in their lofty station without pillars, though no more wondrous than the stability of the earth in its station, both are in need of Him, the Exalted, standing in their places by His power and will through causes (أسباب) specific to them by His permission. Even if the heavens were raised relying on erected pillars, that would not render them independent of the need for Him, the Exalted, and the necessity of His power and will. All things, in all their states, are in absolute need (احتياجاً مطلقاً) of Him, the Exalted—a need that never leaves them, in any state.

However, man—while he perceives the universal law of causality (قانون العلية الكلي) and acknowledges the need of contingent events for originating causes, and while his primordial nature (فطرته) seeks the causes of events and possible matters—when he finds some events coupled with their causes and this is repeated to his senses, it satisfies him. He no longer wonders at witnessing them in that state, nor does he investigate them. But when he sees heavy bodies falling to the earth, and then finds a roof raised above the earth that does not fall upon it, he wonders and investigates until he finds the supports or pillars upon which the roof stands. At that point, despite the repetition to the senses, his soul ceases to investigate every instance where he witnesses something lofty relying on pillars or supports.

But if he finds a matter that breaks this familiar habit, such as the celestial bodies standing in their thickness without a pillar to rely on, or the birds spreading their wings and folding them, then his soul is drawn to investigate the efficient cause (السبب الفاعل) for it, like one awakened from sleep.

Thus, His saying, the Exalted: **رَفَعَ السَّماواتِ بِغَيْرِ عَمَدٍ تَرَوْنَها** — "raised the heavens without any pillars that you may see" — describes the heavens as being "without pillars that you may see" not to indicate the absolute negation of pillars—such that "that you may see" would be an explanatory description with no counter-implication—nor to indicate the negation of perceptible pillars. In both cases, it would imply that since they have no pillars, Allah, the Glorified, is the Raiser and Sustainer of them without the mediation of a cause. If they had pillars like other things that rely on support, the pillars would be the raisers and sustainers without need for Allah, as is often the delusion of the common folk—that what is attributed to Allah are those things whose causes are unknown, like celestial matters, atmospheric events, the soul, and the like.

For His speech, the Exalted, stipulates, firstly, that everything to which the term "thing" applies, other than Allah, is a creation of Allah, and no creation or command is devoid of attribution to Him, as He said: **اللهُ خالِقُ كُلِّ شَيْءٍ** — "Allah is the creator of all things" (13:16), and He said: **أَلا لَهُ الْخَلْقُ وَالْأَمْرُ** — "Look! All creation and command belong to Him" (7:54).

Secondly, it stipulates that the sunnah of causes (سنة الأسباب) is continuous and constant, and that He, the Exalted, is on a straight path. There is no sense in the law of causes applying to some physical matters and not others—such that some events, like earthly ones, are attributed to Him through the mediation of causes, while others, like celestial matters, are attributed to Him without mediation. If a roof, for example, stands on a pillar, it stands by a cause specific to it by the permission of Allah; and if a celestial body stands without a pillar, it also stands by a cause specific to it, such as its specific nature or universal gravitation, by the permission of Allah.

Rather, He qualified the raising of the heavens with the phrase "without pillars that you may see" to alert the primordial nature (فطرة) of people and awaken it, so it may be drawn to investigate the cause, which inevitably leads to Allah, the Glorified. He followed a similar path in His saying in the following verse: **وَهُوَ الَّذِي مَدَّ الْأَرْضَ وَجَعَلَ فِيها رَواسِيَ وَأَنْهاراً** — "It is He who has spread out the earth and established in it mountains and rivers" (13:3), as we shall clarify.

Since the objective in this context—as guided by the flow of the verses—is the monotheism of Lordship (توحيد الربوبية) and the demonstration that Allah, the Glorified, is the Lord of everything and there is no Lord besides Him, not the primary proof of the Creator, He followed His saying: **رَفَعَ السَّماواتِ** — "raised the heavens" — with His saying: **ثُمَّ اسْتَوى عَلَى الْعَرْشِ وَسَخَّرَ الشَّمْسَ وَالْقَمَرَ** — "then He settled on the Throne and He disposed the sun and the moon" (13:2). This indicates the general governance (التدبير العام) which is unified by the connection of its parts to one another, to prove thereby that the Lord of all and their Master, the Governor of their affairs, is One.

This is because the idolaters (الوثنية) whom the Quran debates do not deny that the Creator of all and its Originator is One, having no partner in His creation and origination, and that is Allah, the Glorified. Rather, they believe that He delegated the governance (تدبير) of every affair of the cosmos and every species—such as the earth, the sky, man, animals, land, war, peace, life, and death—to one of the powerful beings. Thus, they believe these beings should be worshipped to gain their benefit and avoid their harm. Therefore, nothing avails in refuting them except restricting Lordship (الربوبية) to Allah, the Glorified, and proving that He is the Lord and there is no Lord besides Him. As for the monotheism of Divinity (توحيد الألوهية) in the sense of proving that the Necessary Being (الواجب الوجود) is One and there is no necessary being besides Him, and to Him all existence returns—this is a matter the idolaters do not deny, nor does it trouble them.

From here it becomes clear that His saying: **الَّذِي رَفَعَ السَّماواتِ بِغَيْرِ عَمَدٍ تَرَوْنَها** — "who raised the heavens without any pillars that you may see" — is placed at the beginning of the verse as a preamble and preparation for His saying: **ثُمَّ اسْتَوى عَلَى الْعَرْشِ** — "then He settled on the Throne" — without being the primary intent in the presented argument. The relation of this opening to its conclusion is like the relation of the opening to the conclusion in His saying: **إِنَّ رَبَّكُمُ اللهُ الَّذِي خَلَقَ السَّماواتِ وَالْأَرْضَ فِي سِتَّةِ أَيَّامٍ ثُمَّ اسْتَوى عَلَى الْعَرْشِ** — "Indeed your Lord is Allah, who created the heavens and the earth in six days, and then settled on the Throne" (7:54, 10:3), and similar verses.

It also appears that the phrase **بِغَيْرِ عَمَدٍ** — "without pillars" — is connected to "raised," and **تَرَوْنَها** — "that you may see" — is a description of the pillars. The meaning is that He raised them without perceptible, visible pillars. As for the view of those who make **تَرَوْنَها** a new sentence (جملة مستأنفة) to repel an objection—as if the listener, upon hearing "He raised the heavens without pillars," asked: "What is the proof for that?" and was answered: "You see them," meaning the proof is that they are visible to you—this is far-fetched. Unless "the heavens" is taken to mean the entire upward direction including the celestial bodies of stars, planets, the air accumulated above the earth, and the clouds, for all of these are raised without pillars and are visible to man.

His saying: **ثُمَّ اسْتَوى عَلَى الْعَرْشِ وَسَخَّرَ الشَّمْسَ وَالْقَمَرَ** — "then He settled on the Throne and He disposed the sun and the moon." The discussion regarding the meaning of the Throne (العرش), settling (الاستواء), and subjugation (التسخير) has already preceded in the commentary on Surah al-A'raf, verse 54.

His saying: **كُلٌّ يَجْرِي لِأَجَلٍ مُسَمًّى** — "each moving for a specified term." That is, each of them moves toward a certain term at which it stops and does not exceed. So it has been said. It is possible, indeed preferable, that the omitted pronoun is a plural pronoun referring to all, meaning: each of the heavens, the sun, and the moon moves toward a specified term, for the law of movement and motion is universal and constant in all these bodies.

The discussion regarding the meaning of the "specified term" (الأجل المسمى) has already preceded in the commentary on Surah al-An'am, verse 1.

His saying: **يُدَبِّرُ الْأَمْرَ** — "He directs the command." Governance (التدبير) is to bring a thing after another. It refers to the arrangement of multiple and diverse things and their organization by placing each thing in its specific place, such that each attains the intended purpose and benefit, and the state is not disrupted by the disintegration of the origin or the corruption and conflict of the parts. It is said: "He governed the affairs of the house," meaning he organized its matters and the actions pertaining to it such that it led to the well-being of its state and its inhabitants' enjoyment of the desired benefits.

Thus, the governance of the world's affairs is the organization of its parts in a sound and firm manner, such that everything is directed toward its intended goal—which is the ultimate specific perfection possible for it and the end of the specified term toward which it is driven. The governance of the whole is the execution of the general cosmic system such that it is directed toward its universal goal, which is the return to Allah and the manifestation of the Hereafter after this world.

His saying: **يُفَصِّلُ الْآياتِ لَعَلَّكُمْ بِلِقاءِ رَبِّكُمْ تُوقِنُونَ** — "He elaborates the signs that you may be certain of the encounter with your Lord." The apparent context is that the "signs" (الآيات) refer to the cosmic signs. Thus, their "elaboration" (تفصيل) is the distinguishing of some from others and their unweaving after being woven together. This is part of His sunnah, the Exalted: He elaborates things, distinguishes each thing from every other thing, and brings out from each thing what is latent and hidden within it. Thus, light is separated from darkness, truth from falsehood, good from evil, the righteous from the corrupt, and the rewarded from the criminal.

Therefore, He followed it with His saying: **لَعَلَّكُمْ بِلِقاءِ رَبِّكُمْ تُوقِنُونَ** — "that you may be certain of the encounter with your Lord." For the Day of Encounter is the Hour which Allah has named the Day of Decision (يوم الفصل), and in which He promised the distinction of the God-conscious from the criminals and the profligates. He said: **إِنَّ يَوْمَ الْفَصْلِ مِيقاتُهُمْ أَجْمَعِينَ** — "Indeed the Day of Decision is the tryst for them all" (44:40), and He said: **وَامْتازُوا الْيَوْمَ أَيُّهَا الْمُجْرِمُونَ** — "‘Get apart today, you guilty ones!’" (36:59), and He said: **لِيَمِيزَ اللهُ الْخَبِيثَ مِنَ الطَّيِّبِ وَيَجْعَلَ الْخَبِيثَ بَعْضَهُ عَلى بَعْضٍ فَيَرْكُمَهُ جَمِيعاً فَيَجْعَلَهُ فِي جَهَنَّمَ أُولئِكَ هُمُ الْخاسِرُونَ** — "so that Allah may separate the bad from the good, and put the bad on top of one another, and heap them up together, and put them into hell. It is they who are the losers" (8:37).

The more common view among exegetes is that the "signs" refer to the verses of the Books revealed from Allah. Thus, their "elaboration" for such a purpose means their explanation and disclosure through clarification in the Books revealed to the prophets of Allah, so that people may contemplate them, reflect, and understand. For in that is the hope that they may be certain of the encounter with Allah and the return to Him. However, the meaning we have presented is more clearly necessary and more closely aligned with the context.

In His saying: **لَعَلَّكُمْ بِلِقاءِ رَبِّكُمْ** — "that you may be certain of the encounter with your Lord" — rather than saying "of His encounter," the explicit noun is used in place of the pronoun. The reason for this is the insistence on establishing Lordship (الربوبية), emphasizing it, and indicating that He who created the world and governed its affairs, thereby becoming its Lord, is also your Lord. Thus, there is no Lord but one Lord, having no partner.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **وَهُوَ الَّذِي مَدَّ الْأَرْضَ وَجَعَلَ فِيها رَواسِيَ وَأَنْهاراً** — "It is He who has spread out the earth and established in it mountains and rivers..." (13:3). *Rawasi* (رواسي) is the plural of *rasiyah* (راسية), from *rasa* (رسا) when something becomes firm and settled; it refers to the mountains due to their firmness in their station. *Zawj* (الزوج) is the opposite of *fard* (الفرد) — *single*; it is applied to the pair together and to one of them. Sometimes "two pairs" (زوجان) is qualified by "two" (اثنان) for emphasis to indicate that two are intended, not four, as in the verse.

His saying: **هُوَ الَّذِي مَدَّ الْأَرْضَ** — "It is He who has spread out the earth." That is, He expanded it in a manner suitable for animals to live upon and for crops and trees to grow. The discussion regarding the attribution of the earth's spreading to Him, the Exalted, and its serving as a preamble for what follows—His saying: **وَجَعَلَ فِيها رَواسِيَ وَأَنْهاراً** — is similar to the discussion regarding His saying in the previous verse: **اللهُ الَّذِي رَفَعَ السَّماواتِ بِغَيْرِ عَمَدٍ تَرَوْنَها**.

His saying: **وَجَعَلَ فِيها رَواسِيَ وَأَنْهاراً** — "and established in it mountains and rivers." The pronoun refers to the earth. The speech is structured such that its parts follow one another. The purpose—and Allah knows best—is to explain His governance (تدبيره) of the affairs of the earth's inhabitants, both humans and animals, in their movement to seek sustenance and their stillness for rest. Allah, the Glorified, spread the earth, and had He not done so, it would not have been suitable for the survival of the human and animal species. And had it been merely spread without elevation and depression in its surface, it would not have been suitable for the emergence of the water treasures stored within it onto its surface to water crops and orchards. Thus, He, the Glorified, established in it firm mountains (الجبال الرواسي), stored within them the heavenly water that descends upon the earth, carved rivers from their sides, and caused springs to gush forth overlooking the plains to water the crops and gardens. Thereby, diverse fruits emerge—sweet and bitter, summer and winter, wild and domestic. He also gave the night and day dominion over the face of the earth; they are two powerful factors in the growth of fruits and produce by the application of heat and cold, which affect ripening, growth, expansion, and contraction, and by the application of light and darkness, which organize the movement of animals and humans in their quest for sustenance and their stillness for sleep and rest.

Thus, the spreading of the earth facilitates the placement of firm mountains, which leads to the carving of rivers, which leads to the production of diverse pairs of fruits. Through the night and day, the objective is completed. In all of this is a continuous and unified governance that reveals a single Wise Governor, having no partner in His Lordship. Indeed, in that are signs for a people who reflect.

His saying: **وَمِنْ كُلِّ الثَّمَراتِ جَعَلَ فِيها زَوْجَيْنِ اثْنَيْنِ** — "and of every fruit He has placed in it two pairs." That is, from all possible fruits, He has placed on earth diverse types—one type differing from another, such as summer and winter, sweet and otherwise, fresh and dry.

This is the well-known interpretation of "two pairs" (زوجين اثنين). The "two pairs" refer to a category (صنف) being differed from by another category, whether they are two categories with no third or not. This is similar to where the dual is used for repetition, as in His saying, the Exalted: **ثُمَّ ارْجِعِ الْبَصَرَ كَرَّتَيْنِ** — "Then look again and again" (67:4), where returning the gaze time after time is intended, however numerous it may be.

In the commentary *al-Jawahir*, regarding His saying: **زَوْجَيْنِ اثْنَيْنِ**, it is said: "He placed in it, from all types of fruits, two pairs: male and female in their blossoms at the time of their formation. Modern discovery has shown that no tree or crop produces its fruit or grain except from between two: male and female."

The male organ may be with the female organ in a single tree, as in most trees, or the male organ may be in one tree and the other in another tree, like the date palm. Where both organs are in one tree, they may either be together in a single blossom, or each may be in a blossom alone. The latter is like the pumpkin, and the former is like the cotton plant, where the male organ is with the female organ in a single blossom. End quote.

What he mentioned, though it is among the scientific truths about which there is no doubt, is not supported by the apparent meaning of the noble verse. For its apparent meaning is that the fruits themselves are "two pairs," not that they are created from "two pairs." Had that been the intent, it would have been more appropriate to say: "and of all fruits He made in it *from* two pairs."

Yes, there is no harm in deriving this from verses such as His saying, the Exalted: **سُبْحانَ الَّذِي خَلَقَ الْأَزْواجَ كُلَّها مِمَّا تُنْبِتُ الْأَرْضُ** — "Immaculate is He who has created all the pairs of what the earth grows..." (36:36), and His saying: **وَأَنْزَلْنا مِنَ السَّماءِ ماءً فَأَنْبَتْنا فِيها مِنْ كُلِّ زَوْجٍ كَرِيمٍ** — "and sent down water from the sky, and caused every splendid kind [of plant] to grow in it" (31:10), and His saying: **وَمِنْ كُلِّ شَيْءٍ خَلَقْنا زَوْجَيْنِ لَعَلَّكُمْ تَذَكَّرُونَ** — "In all things We have created pairs so that you may take admonition" (51:49).

Some have mentioned that "two pairs" refers to male and female, sweet and sour, and all other categories, such that the "two pairs" would be four individuals: male and female, each differing by attributes that are more than one, such as sweet and otherwise, summer and otherwise. This is as you see.

His saying: **يُغْشِي اللَّيْلَ النَّهارَ** — "He draws the night's cover over the day." That is, He clothes the light of day with the darkness of night, so the air becomes dark after it was bright. Some have mentioned that it means each of the night and day covers the other, and the night follows the day and the day follows the night, but there is no contextual clue (قرينة) to indicate this.

Then He concluded the verse with His saying: **إِنَّ في ذلِكَ لَآياتٍ لِقَوْمٍ يَتَفَكَّرُونَ** — "There are indeed signs in that for a people who reflect." For reflecting upon the system functioning within them, which governs them and dictates the connection and harmony of their parts—leading to the direction of the whole and every part toward specific goals—reveals their connection to a single intellectual governance (تدبير واحد عقلي) of utmost precision and firmness. This indicates that they have one Lord, having no partner in His Lordship, All-Knowing (عليماً) untouched by ignorance, All-Powerful (قديراً) whose power is never overcome, possessing care (عناية) for everything, especially for man, driving him toward that which contains his eternal happiness.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **وَفِي الْأَرْضِ قِطَعٌ مُتَجاوِراتٌ وَجَنَّاتٌ مِنْ أَعْنابٍ وَزَرْعٌ وَنَخِيلٌ صِنْوانٌ وَغَيْرُ صِنْوانٍ** — "In the earth are neighboring terrains [some fertile and others barren] and gardens of vines, and crops and date palms, growing from one root or from different roots..." (13:4). Al-Raghib said: "*al-Sinw* (الصنو) is the branch emerging from the root of the tree. It is said: 'They are the two *sinws* of a date palm,' and 'So-and-so is the *sinw* of his father.' The dual is *sinwan* (صنوان) and the plural is *sinwan* (صنوان). He, the Exalted, said: **صِنْوانٌ وَغَيْرُ صِنْوانٍ**." End quote. He also said: "*al-Ukul* (الأكل) refers to what is eaten, with a *dammah* or *sukun* on the *kaf*. He, the Exalted, said: **أُكُلُها دائِمٌ** — 'its fruit is everlasting' (13:35). *al-Aklah* (الأكلة) is for a single instance, and *al-uklah* (الأكلة) is like a morsel." End quote.

The meaning is that among the proofs that this functioning system is established by the governance of a Governor behind it—to whom things are subject by their natures and who conducts them as He wills and how He wills—is that in the earth are neighboring terrains (قطع متجاورات), close to one another and similar in the nature of their soil. Yet in them are gardens of vines—and grapes are among the fruits that differ greatly in shape, color, taste, size, delicacy, quality, and so on. In them are crops differing in their genus and species, such as wheat, barley, and others. In them are date palms, some *sinwan*—meaning identical ones growing from a common root—and others *ghayru sinwan*—meaning separate ones. All are watered by a single water, yet We favor some over others in the desired excellence of their attributes.

If it is said: "These differences are due to their specific natures or the external factors that act upon them, affecting their shapes, colors, and other attributes, as detailed in scientific research concerning their natures, properties, and the factors influencing their formation and attributes."

It is replied: Yes, but the question then shifts to the cause of the difference in these internal natures and factors. What is the cause of their difference that leads to the difference in effects? Ultimately, it ends at the matter (المادة) common to all, whose parts are similar. Such a thing is not suitable to account for this witnessed difference. Thus, there must be a cause above these causes that created the common matter, then created within it whatever forms and effects He willed. In other words, there is a single cause possessing consciousness and will, to whose differing wills these differences are attributed. Without it, nothing would be distinguished from anything else, nor would anything differ.

It is necessary for the researcher contemplating these verses to realize that attributing the difference in creation to the difference in the will of Allah, the Glorified, is not an invalidation of the law of cause and effect (قانون العلة والمعلول), as might be imagined. For the will of Allah, the Glorified, is not an attribute accidental to His essence like our will, such that His essence would change with the change of wills. Rather, these differing wills are an attribute of His act (صفة فعله) and are derived from the complete causes (العلل التامة) of things. Let this point remain with you in summary until its clarification reaches you in a suitable place, if Allah the Almighty wills.

Since the argument is built upon intellectual premises (مقدمات عقلية) that are not complete without them, He followed it with His saying: **إِنَّ فِي ذلِكَ لَآياتٍ لِقَوْمٍ يَعْقِلُونَ** — "There are indeed signs in that for a people who exercise their reason."

It has become clear from the preceding explanation that the attribution of the difference in fruit (الأكل) to Him, the Exalted, without mentioning the intermediary or intermediaries, is like the attribution of raising the sky without visible pillars, spreading the earth, and establishing mountains and rivers to Him, the Exalted, by omitting the intermediaries. The purpose of this is to alert the primordial nature of the listeners so it may be drawn to investigate the cause of the differences, ending ultimately with Allah, the Mightiest of causes.

In the verse, there is a subtle shift (التفات) from the third person to the first person plural, which is in His saying, the Exalted: **وَنُفَضِّلُ بَعْضَها عَلى بَعْضٍ فِي الْأُكُلِ** — "and We favor some of them over others in fruit." Perhaps the point in this is to identify the true cause with the briefest explanation, as if it were said: "And some are favored over others in fruit, and the one who favors is none but Allah, the Glorified." Then the Speaker identified Himself and manifested with the term of magnification that He is the cause sought by researchers, and to His presence this favoring ends. Then He summarized this favoring by saying: **وَنُفَضِّلُ بَعْضَها عَلى بَعْضٍ فِي الْأُكُلِ**. The expression in the first person plural is not devoid of an indication that there are divine causes below Allah, the Glorified, acting by His command and ending with Him, the Glorified.

It has become clear from what preceded that the verse was presented as an argument for the monotheism of Lordship (توحيد الربوبية), not for proving the Creator or the monotheism of the Essence. Its summary is that the difference of effects in things despite the unity of origin reveals their attribution to a cause beyond the common, unified nature, and their organization according to His will and governance. Thus, the Governor of them is Allah, the Glorified, and He is their Lord; there is no Lord besides Him. Therefore, what appears from some exegetes that the verse is presented to prove the Creator is misplaced.

Furthermore, the verses, as appears from their context, are presented to argue against the idolaters. They only deny the unity of Lordship and affirm various lords, while acknowledging the unity of the essence of the True Necessary Being, exalted is His Name. Thus, there is no sense in arguing against them with what results in the world having a Creator. Some have noticed this and mentioned that the verse is an argument against the materialists (دهرية) among the Arabs who denied the existence of the Creator, but this is refuted by the fact that there is no evidence from the context of the verses for what he claimed.

It has also become clear that the difference between the two arguments—that in His saying: **وَهُوَ الَّذِي مَدَّ الْأَرْضَ** — "It is He who has spread out the earth" — and that in His saying: **وَفِي الْأَرْضِ قِطَعٌ مُتَجاوِراتٌ** — "In the earth are neighboring terrains" — is that the first proceeds from the path of unity in multiplicity (الوحدة في الكثرة) and the connection and continuity in the governance pertaining to these diverse things, which leads to the unity of their Governor. The second proceeds from the path of multiplicity in unity (الكثرة في الوحدة) and the difference of effects and properties in things that have a single origin, which reveals that the Originator who bestows these diverse and scattered effects and properties is a matter beyond their natures and a cause above these causes that return to a single origin—and He is the Lord of all, there is no Lord besides Him.

As for the first argument mentioned before these two, namely His saying, the Exalted: **اللهُ الَّذِي رَفَعَ السَّماواتِ** — "It is Allah who raised the heavens" — it is as if it proceeds from both paths together. For it mentions governance (التدبير), which contains the unification of the many and the gathering of scattered matters; and elaboration (التفصيل), which contains the multiplication of the one and the separation of gathered things. Its summary is that the affair of the world, despite its fragmentation and scattering, is under a single governance, so it has one Lord, who is Allah, the Glorified. And He, the Exalted, elaborates the signs, distinguishing each thing from every other thing, thus separating the happy from the wretched and truth from falsehood—which is the Return (المعاد). Therefore, He derived from it both Lordship and the Return together, as He said: **لَعَلَّكُمْ بِلِقاءِ رَبِّكُمْ تُوقِنُونَ** — "that you may be certain of the encounter with your Lord."

## (Narrative Discussion)

In *Tafsir al-Ayyashi*, from al-Khattab al-A'war, attributed to the people of knowledge and jurisprudence from the Family of Muhammad (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم), he said: "**وَفِي الْأَرْضِ قِطَعٌ مُتَجاوِراتٌ** — 'In the earth are neighboring terrains' — means this good land neighbors this salty land, yet it is not of it, just as a people neighbor a people yet are not of them."

In *Tafsir al-Burhan*, from Ibn Shahr Ashub, from al-Kharkushi in *Sharaf al-Mustafa*, al-Tha'labi in *al-Kashf wa al-Bayan*, and al-Fadl ibn Shadhan in *al-Amali*—and the wording is his—with his chain of narration from Jabir ibn Abdullah, who said: "I heard the Messenger of Allah (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) saying to Ali (عليه السلام): **الناس من شجرة شتى ـ وأنا وأنت من شجرة واحدة** — 'People are from diverse trees, but you and I are from a single tree.' Then he recited: **جَنَّاتٌ مِنْ أَعْنابٍ وَزَرْعٌ وَنَخِيلٌ صِنْوانٌ وَغَيْرُ صِنْوانٍ ـ يُسْقى بِماءٍ واحِدٍ** — 'gardens of vines, and crops and date palms, growing from one root or from different roots, watered by a single water' (13:4)—by the Prophet and by you." He said: "And al-Natanzi narrated it in *al-Khasa'is* from Salman." In another narration: "I and Ali are from one tree, and people are from diverse trees."

The author of *al-Burhan* said: "The hadith of Jabir ibn Abdullah was also narrated by al-Tabarsi and Ali ibn Isa in *Kashf al-Ghummah*."

**I say:** It was narrated in *al-Durr al-Manthur* from al-Hakim and Ibn Mardawayh from Jabir, who said: "I heard the Messenger of Allah (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) saying: 'O Ali, people are from diverse trees, but you and I, O Ali, are from a single tree.' Then the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) recited: **وَجَنَّاتٌ مِنْ أَعْنابٍ وَزَرْعٌ وَنَخِيلٌ ـ صِنْوانٌ وَغَيْرُ صِنْوانٍ**."

In *al-Durr al-Manthur*, al-Tirmidhi (who graded it *hasan*), al-Bazzar, Ibn Jarir, Ibn al-Mundhir, Abu al-Shaykh, and Ibn Mardawayh narrated from Abu Hurayrah, from the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) regarding His saying, the Exalted: **وَنُفَضِّلُ بَعْضَها عَلى بَعْضٍ فِي الْأُكُلِ** — "and We favor some of them over others in fruit." He said: "The *daqal* (الدقل), the *farisi* (الفارسي), the sweet, and the sour." (1)

---
(1) *al-Daqal* is the black date, and it seems the *farisi* is a good type of it.
